title: The King of Glory Comes In
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 03-24-24 - The King of Glory Comes In - Luke 19_28-44 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Context from the Parable of the Pounds
subpoints: Luke 19:11-27 — the parable of the pounds immediately precedes the triumphal entry; the crowd expected the kingdom to appear immediately
The parable warns of judgment on those who reject the king — setting up the entry and Jesus's weeping over Jerusalem
Jesus is orchestrating events deliberately: timing his entry to maximize exposure and to bring his enemies' timetable in sync with his own

outline.title: 1. The Fulfillment of Zechariah 9
subpoints: Zechariah 9:9-10: 'Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion... your king is coming to you; he is just and having salvation, lowly and riding on a donkey, a colt, the foal of a donkey'
Jesus rode a colt that had never been ridden — a wild, unbroken animal; that it submitted without incident is a display of divine power and sovereignty
The people who followed from Jericho were carrying palm branches — there are no palm trees near Jerusalem; the palms had been carried for miles, anticipating this moment
Every detail of the entry is orchestrated: the specific animal, the specific road, the specific crowd

outline.title: 2. Jesus Weeping Over Jerusalem
subpoints: Luke 19:41-44: as Jesus drew near, he wept over the city — the strongest Greek word for weeping, convulsive sobbing
He weeps because Jerusalem does not know 'the things that make for peace' and did not recognize 'the time of your visitation'
The prophecy is precise: enemies will build a siege wall, encircle Jerusalem, tear it down, not leave one stone on another — fulfilled in 70 AD by Titus
Jesus weeps not because he failed but because of the tragedy of those who refuse the king who comes to save them

outline.title: 3. Applications: Three Calls from the Triumphal Entry
subpoints: Don't waste opportunities to believe: Jerusalem was given its moment of visitation and did not recognize it; God's appointed time for each person will not recur indefinitely
Give what Christ asks: the disciples gave the colt without knowing why, at his mere word; obedience does not require full understanding
Answer the question 'Who is this?': the crowd asked it (Matthew 21:10); the whole of history turns on the answer; Jesus is the king who comes to save and to judge

summary: A Palm Sunday sermon on Luke 19:28-44. The triumphal entry is set in context by the immediately preceding parable of the pounds, which warns of judgment on those who reject the king. Jesus rides into Jerusalem on an unbroken colt, fulfilling Zechariah 9:9-10 with precision — even the palm branches were carried from distant Jericho, since no palms grew near Jerusalem. Jesus is deliberately orchestrating maximum public exposure, aligning his enemies' timetable with his own. He weeps over Jerusalem with convulsive grief, prophesying the precise destruction of 70 AD, because the city does not recognize 'the time of its visitation.' Three applications close the sermon: don't waste the opportunity to believe; give whatever Christ asks without needing full understanding; and answer the decisive question — 'Who is this?'
transcript:
## Introduction: The Parable of the Pounds

Before Jesus enters Jerusalem in Luke 19, he tells the parable of the pounds — and it matters that the two are connected. The disciples and the crowd expected the kingdom of God to appear immediately. So Jesus tells them a parable: a nobleman goes into a far country to receive for himself a kingdom. His servants are given resources and told to do business until he returns. And then there are those who say: 'We will not have this man to reign over us.'

The nobleman returns, rewards his faithful servants — and judges those who rejected him. The parable warns us plainly: when the king comes and you reject him, judgment follows. That is the frame through which Luke wants us to see the triumphal entry.

## 1. The Fulfillment of Zechariah

Jesus sends two disciples ahead to find a colt tied at a specific place. No one has ever ridden it. When the owners ask why the disciples are untying it, they say: 'The Lord has need of it.' And the owners let it go.

All of this is fulfillment. Zechariah 9:9-10: *Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion! Shout, O daughter of Jerusalem! Behold, your King is coming to you; he is just and having salvation, lowly and riding on a donkey, a colt, the foal of a donkey.* Not a war horse — a donkey. Not a conquering general — a humble king.

But here is something that tends to get overlooked: this colt had never been ridden. It was an unbroken animal. And yet it submitted to Jesus without a moment's resistance. That is a quiet but unmistakable display of power. The one who created all things, including this animal, commands it — and it obeys.

The crowd that came with Jesus from Jericho was waving palm branches. That detail is easy to miss. There are no palm trees near Jerusalem. The road from Jericho to Jerusalem climbs 3,300 feet through the Judean desert. These people had carried palm branches for miles, in anticipation. They were ready. They had been waiting for this.

Jesus is orchestrating this entrance with deliberate precision. He times everything to bring maximum public attention — knowing that this exposure is part of his plan to bring his enemies' timetable into alignment with his own. He will die at Passover. He will not die before Passover. He controls every detail.

## 2. Jesus Weeps Over Jerusalem

Verse 41: *As he drew near, he saw the city and wept over it.*

The word translated 'wept' here is the strongest word in the Greek for weeping — not a quiet shedding of tears but convulsive, heaving, audible sobbing. In the midst of the crowd's praise, the King is weeping.

He says: *If you had known, even you, especially in this your day, the things that make for your peace! But now they are hidden from your eyes.* The city has its moment. The King has come. But it will not see.

And then the prophecy: *Days will come upon you when your enemies will build an embankment around you, surround you and close you in on every side, and level you, and your children within you, to the ground; and they will not leave in you one stone upon another, because you did not know the time of your visitation.*

This was fulfilled in 70 AD when the Roman general Titus besieged Jerusalem for five months. He built a wall around the entire city — exactly as Jesus described. A million people died of starvation and violence. The temple was razed. Not one stone was left on another.

Jesus weeps not because the plan failed but because of the weight of the tragedy — people rejecting the only one who could save them, and suffering what rejection brings.

## 3. Applications

Three things this passage calls us to.

First: don't waste the opportunity to believe. Jerusalem had its day of visitation, and it did not know. God appoints times and seasons. He is extraordinarily patient. But the moment of visitation is not indefinite. If you have been sitting on the question of what to do with Jesus, hear his weeping.

Second: give what Christ asks. The disciples gave the colt without knowing exactly why. 'The Lord has need of it' was sufficient. They didn't have full information. They trusted the word of the Lord and gave what he asked. The unplanned, uncontrolled life keeps everything for itself. The disciple gives freely.

Third: answer the question. As Jesus entered Jerusalem, the city stirred and people asked: *Who is this?* (Matthew 21:10). That question stands over all of history. Who is this? He is the king who comes in the name of the Lord — the king who weeps over those who reject him, the king who will die for those who receive him, the king who will return to judge the living and the dead. Who is this? Everything turns on how you answer.

Father, as we come to this week — this great week that ends at an empty tomb — open our eyes to see clearly who Jesus is and what he has done. Help us not to miss the time of our visitation. In his name, amen.
